title: Living i Boulder vlog 3 - 15 - 2018: Bike Racer Levels Pages

enrichment.summary: Kyle vlogs from Boulder on March 15, 2018 about the tedious work of building out bike racer levels pages for his website, converting units and enforcing data consistency across hundreds of pages. He spins out into broader reflections on slow computers limiting web design, his philosophy of long single-page scrollable sites over paginated ones, and predictions that faster computers and voice input will transform personal websites.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Worth extreme tedium — must get data format right the first time since numbers can't be bulk-changed like CSS
Underutilized and superior; entire books should live on one page
The most amazing tool for navigating long content; claims only his website supports it properly
Faster computers and voice input will make building massive personal websites trivially easy within ~10 years
Page-per-section is a workaround for slow computers and ad-count rules, not good design

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I have been working on my website, dividing up levels from different categories and businesses with data from England and America.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I couldn't find enough data from Canada and Australia for my website.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: My computer overheats when processing large amounts of data for my website.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I've been converting miles and hours to farmers and hours, which is super tedious.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I am meticulous about consistency in my website to ensure information is displayed uniformly across all pages.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Making changes to the design of my website is easier with CSS, but some elements require individual adjustments.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I have to be decisive at the beginning of my work to minimize the need for redoing tasks later.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I made progress on my website, but some tasks, like adjusting spacing in titles, took a lot of time due to my slow computer.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: My website is pushing my computer to its limits with the amount of data it processes.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I believe the future of web design will involve faster computers that can handle large pages effortlessly, possibly even allowing voice commands for content creation.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: In the future, building large websites like mine could take just a day with advanced computer technology.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I think personal websites will become more popular as technology makes them easier to create and maintain.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Scrolling is an underutilized feature in web design that should be used more, especially for large pages like books.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The 'Control F' function is an amazing tool for navigating large pages and finding specific content quickly.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I am collecting books and organizing them by decades, starting from the 20th century and moving backward.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Reading about historical events often leads me to interesting discoveries and Wikipedia rabbit holes.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I want to implement a grading system for the content on my website, but it will take time.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I believe that in the future, websites like Wikipedia will be commonplace as more people contribute information online.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Today, I plan to continue working on my website, focusing on making all the data consistent.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I have figured out the math for converting units on my website, which is simple division.
